text: Step 4: Select a number of repeating variables, where the number required is equal to the number of reference dimensions ( \( \mathbf{K} \) ). (')All of the reference dimensions must be included in the group of repeating variables, and they should be dimensionally independent of one another. \( { }^{13} \) Never choose the dependent variable to be the repeating variable and never choose two repeating variables with the same dimensions! Step 5: Form the Pi terms by multiplying one nonrepeating variable by the product of the repeating variables each raised to an unknown exponent. Determine the exponents so that the Pi term is dimensionless. Step 6: Repeat Step 5 for each of the remaining nonrepeating variables. 1-43

(')All of the reference dimensions must be included in the group of repeating variables, and they should be dimensionally independent of one another. \( { }^{13} \) Never choose the dependent variable to be the repeating variable and never choose two repeating variables with the same dimensions! Step 5: Form the Pi terms by multiplying one nonrepeating variable by the product of the repeating variables each raised to an unknown exponent. Determine the exponents so that the Pi term is dimensionless. Step 6: Repeat Step 5 for each of the remaining nonrepeating variables. 1-43

text: Step 7: Check all the Pi terms to make sure they are dimensionless. Simply substitute the dimensions of the variables into the Pi terms. If the result is not dimensionless, you have made a mistake! Step 8: Express the functional form as a relationship among the Pi terms and think about what it means. \( \Pi_{1} \) should contain the dependent variable in the numerator. \[ \boldsymbol{\pi}_{1}=\boldsymbol{f}_{2}\left(\boldsymbol{\Pi}_{2}, \boldsymbol{\Pi}_{3}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\Pi}_{N-K}\right) \leftarrow \] * The advantage of deopate Buckingham Pi theorem is that it can greatly reduce the number of parameters to describe a problem. The actual functional relationship must be determined by experiments. 1-44

The actual functional relationship must be determined by experiments. 1-44

text: Strong explosion Based on his theory developed in 1941, Sir G.I. Taylor inferred the energy of the Trinity nuclear blast based on the photographs made public in 1947. (The work was published in 1950. Independently, L.I. Sedov also developed a similar theory). Key assumptions: (1) Instantaneous point energy release abd (2) Strong shock wave (Mach >> 1). (3) Very high temp 8 pressue in fireball compare to atmosphere. Trinity nuclear explosion (July 16, 1945): a - after 25 ms , b - explosion sequence

(3) Very high temp 8 pressue in fireball compare to atmosphere. Trinity nuclear explosion (July 16, 1945): a - after 25 ms , b - explosion sequence

text: From physical intuition: \[ \underline{\underline{R}}=f\left(E, \rho_{0}, t\right) \leftarrow \text { Functional form } \] Step 1: Done \( N=4 \) Step 2: \( R=[z] \quad p_{0}=\left[m / z^{3}\right] \quad E=\left[\frac{m l^{2}}{t^{2}}\right] \quad t:[t] \quad k=3 \) Step 3: \# of \( \pi \) tems: \( N-K=4-3=1 \) Step 4: Non-Repectin: \( R \) Repecting: \( E_{1} \rho_{0}, t \) Step 5: \[ \begin{array}{l} \Pi_{1}=\rho_{0}^{a} E^{b} t^{c} R=\left[m^{0} l^{0} t^{0}\right] \\ \left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a}\left(\frac{m l^{2}}{t^{2}}\right)^{b}(t)^{b}(l)=m^{0} l^{0} t^{0} \\ \left.\begin{array}{l} m: a+b=0 \\ e:-3 a+2 b+1=0 \\ t:-2 b+c=0 \end{array}\right\} \text { Solve to get } \\ a=\frac{1}{5} \\ b=-\frac{1}{5} \\ c=-\frac{2}{5} \\ \pi_{1}=\rho^{1 / 5} E^{-1 / 5} E^{-2 / 5} R \\ \pi_{1}=\left(\frac{\rho}{E t^{2}}\right)^{1 / 5} R=\frac{C}{\uparrow}\left(\frac{\bar{E} t^{2}}{\rho}\right)^{1 / 5} R \\ \text { End } 9 / 3 / 25 \\ \text { constant \# (dimersionles!) } \end{array} \] 1-46 helps compact experimatel dete.
raw_text: From physical intuition: \[ \underline{\underline{R}}=f\left(E, \rho_{0}, t\right) \leftarrow \text { Functional form } \] Step 1: Done \( N=4 \) Step 2: \( R=[z] \quad p_{0}=\left[m / z^{3}\right] \quad E=\left[\frac{m l^{2}}{t^{2}}\right] \quad t:[t] \quad k=3 \) Step 3: \# of \( \pi \) tems: \( N-K=4-3=1 \) Step 4: Non-Repectin: \( R \) Repecting: \( E_{1} \rho_{0}, t \) Step 5: \[ \begin{array}{l} \Pi_{1}=\rho_{0}^{a} E^{b} t^{c} R=\left[m^{0} l^{0} t^{0}\right] \\ \left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a}\left(\frac{m l^{2}}{t^{2}}\right)^{b}(t)^{b}(l)=m^{0} l^{0} t^{0} \\ \left.\begin{array}{l} m: a+b=0 \\ e:-3 a+2 b+1=0 \\ t:-2 b+c=0 \end{array}\right\} \text { Solve to get } \\ a=\frac{1}{5} \\ b=-\frac{1}{5} \\ c=-\frac{2}{5} \\ \pi_{1}=\rho^{1 / 5} E^{-1 / 5} E^{-2 / 5} R \\ \pi_{1}=\left(\frac{\rho}{E t^{2}}\right)^{1 / 5} R=\frac{C}{\uparrow}\left(\frac{\bar{E} t^{2}}{\rho}\right)^{1 / 5} R \\ \text { End } 9 / 3 / 25 \\ \text { constant \# (dimersionles!) } \end{array} \] 1-46 helps compact experimatel dete.

text: \[ R=C\left(\frac{E t^{2}}{\rho_{0}}\right)^{1 / 5} \approx\left(\frac{E t^{2}}{\rho_{0}}\right)^{1 / 5} \] Plotting \( R(t) \) on a log-log plot, the slope should be \( 2 / 5 \), and the intercept would give \( E \) if \( C \) is known. By solving a model shock wave problem, Taylor found for air \( \mathrm{C}=1.033 \approx 1 \). 7.14 to \( 9.74 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~J} \approx 16.8 \) to 22.9 kiloton For the Trinity explosions, Taylor found \( \mathrm{E}=8.05 \times 10131=19.2 \) kilotons of TNT. It was later revealed that the actual energy was 21 kiloton of TNT. Taylor's original data, with time t in milliseconds and radius R in meters \begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|l|} \hline \( t \) & \( R(t) \) & \( t \) & \( R(t) \) & \( t \) & \( R(t) \) & \( t \) & \( R(t) \) & \( t \) & \( R(t) \) \\ \hline 0.10 & 11.1 & 0.80 & 34.2 & 1.50 & 44.4 & 3.53 & 61.1 & 15.0 & 106.5 \\ \hline 0.24 & 19.9 & 0.94 & 36.3 & 1.65 & 46.0 & 3.80 & 62.9 & 25.0 & 130.0 \\ \hline 0.38 & 25.4 & 1.08 & 38.9 & 1.79 & 46.9 & 4.07 & 64.3 & 34.0 & 145.0 \\ \hline 0.52 & 28.8 & 1.22 & 41.0 & 1.93 & 48.7 & 4.34 & 65.6 & 53.0 & 175.0 \\ \hline 0.66 & 31.9 & 1.36 & 42.8 & 3.26 & 59.0 & 4.61 & 67.3 & 62.0 & 185.0 \\ \hline \end{tabular} Taylor, Geoffrey Ingram, "The Formation of a Blast Wave by a Very Intense Explosion I: Theoretical Discussion." Proceedings of the Royal Society of London, Volume 201, Issue 1065, March 1950. Taylor, Geoffrey Ingram, "The Formation of a Blast Wave by a Very Intense Explosion II: The Atomic Explosion of 1945." Proceedings of the Royal Society of London, Volume 201, Issue 1065, March 1950. 1-47 Deakin, Michael, "G.I. Taylor and the Trinity Test." International Journal of Mathematical Education in Science and Technology, Volume 42, No. 8, May 2011.

text: Example: Force coefficient (2-D) \( \quad C_{F}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{q_{\infty} \cdot C}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{\infty} / s_{\infty}^{2} \cdot C} \) Based on physical intuition we expect the resultant force vector for a given 2-D shape to depend on: Fluid density \( \rho_{\infty} \quad \) Freestream velocity \( V_{\infty} \) Body size (chord) \( \quad \subset \) Viscosity \( \mu_{\infty} \) Speed of sound \( a_{\infty} R^{\prime}= \) resultant force Therefore the magnitude of the resultant force per unit span is \[ R^{\prime}=f\left(\rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty}\right) \leftarrow \text { Functioned Form } \] or as \[ f_{2}\left(R^{\prime}, p_{\infty}, v_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty}\right)=0 \] The Buckingham Pi theorem will tell us how many parameters we can form from this set of variables. 1-48
raw_text: Example: Force coefficient (2-D) \( \quad C_{F}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{q_{\infty} \cdot C}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{\infty} / s_{\infty}^{2} \cdot C} \) Based on physical intuition we expect the resultant force vector for a given 2-D shape to depend on: Fluid density \( \rho_{\infty} \quad \) Freestream velocity \( V_{\infty} \) Body size (chord) \( \quad \subset \) Viscosity \( \mu_{\infty} \) Speed of sound \( a_{\infty} R^{\prime}= \) resultant force Therefore the magnitude of the resultant force per unit span is \[ R^{\prime}=f\left(\rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty}\right) \leftarrow \text { Functioned Form } \] or as \[ f_{2}\left(R^{\prime}, p_{\infty}, v_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty}\right)=0 \] The Buckingham Pi theorem will tell us how many parameters we can form from this set of variables. 1-48

text: Follow the steps outlined above to find the non-dimensional parameters \( \Pi_{i} \) Step 1: The dimensional variables are \[ R^{\prime}, \rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty} \] So \( N=6 \) Step 2: What are the fundamental units needed? Denote \( m= \) mass, \( l= \) length, \( t= \) time. \[ \begin{array}{l} {\left[R^{\prime}\right]=\frac{F}{l}=\frac{m \cdot \frac{l}{t^{2}}}{l}=\frac{m}{t^{2}}} \\ {\left[\rho_{\infty}\right]=m / l^{3}} \\ {\left[V_{\infty}\right]=\frac{l}{t}} \end{array} \] \[ \begin{aligned} {\left[\mu_{\infty}\right] } & =\frac{m}{l \cdot t} \\ {\left[a_{\infty}\right] } & =\frac{l}{t} \\ 3^{[c]} & =l \end{aligned} \] 1-49 Step 3: \# of \( \pi \) Terms \( =N-K=6-3=3 \)
raw_text: Follow the steps outlined above to find the non-dimensional parameters \( \Pi_{i} \) Step 1: The dimensional variables are \[ R^{\prime}, \rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty} \] So \( N=6 \) Step 2: What are the fundamental units needed? Denote \( m= \) mass, \( l= \) length, \( t= \) time. \[ \begin{array}{l} {\left[R^{\prime}\right]=\frac{F}{l}=\frac{m \cdot \frac{l}{t^{2}}}{l}=\frac{m}{t^{2}}} \\ {\left[\rho_{\infty}\right]=m / l^{3}} \\ {\left[V_{\infty}\right]=\frac{l}{t}} \end{array} \] \[ \begin{aligned} {\left[\mu_{\infty}\right] } & =\frac{m}{l \cdot t} \\ {\left[a_{\infty}\right] } & =\frac{l}{t} \\ 3^{[c]} & =l \end{aligned} \] 1-49 Step 3: \# of \( \pi \) Terms \( =N-K=6-3=3 \)

text: 4 Step \$: Choose \( K \) repeating variables from among the N dimensional variables. We don't want to choose \( R^{\prime} \) because it is the variable of interest. We don't want to choose both \( a_{\infty} \) and \( V_{\infty} \) because their ratio is a nondimensional parameter. wits, so chouse to be repecting? one to Choose the following as repeating variables: \( \quad \rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c \). Check that we can represent each of the fundamental units with this set of variables: mass: time: \( \frac{c}{V_{\infty}}:\left[\frac{l}{l / t}\right]:[t] \checkmark \) length: \( c=[\ell] \checkmark \) Not typicilly neressery \( t \) do, but a gase way to tru-sleshart if \( \pi \) terms dont make serse! \( 1-50 \)

We don't want to choose both \( a_{\infty} \) and \( V_{\infty} \) because their ratio is a nondimensional parameter. wits, so chouse to be repecting? one to Choose the following as repeating variables: \( \quad \rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c \). Check that we can represent each of the fundamental units with this set of variables: mass: time: \( \frac{c}{V_{\infty}}:\left[\frac{l}{l / t}\right]:[t] \checkmark \) length: \( c=[\ell] \checkmark \) Not typicilly neressery \( t \) do, but a gase way to tru-sleshart if \( \pi \) terms dont make serse! \( 1-50 \)

text: Step 4: Form the non-dimensional products. Let \[ \Pi_{1}=\underbrace{\rho_{\infty}^{a_{11}} V_{\infty}^{a_{12}} c^{a_{13}}}_{\text {Reperty }} \underbrace{R^{\prime}} \text { Nu-Repertis. } \quad \text { Solve for } a_{11}, a_{12}, a_{13} \] \( \Pi_{1} \) is dimensionless, so each exponent must be zero: \[ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a l_{1}}\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{12}}(l)^{a_{13}}\left(\frac{m}{t^{2}}\right)=m^{\circ} l^{\circ} t^{\circ} \\ m: & a_{11}+1=0 \\ l:-3 a_{11}+a_{12}+a_{13}=0 \Longrightarrow & \Rightarrow a_{13}=-1 \quad \pi_{1}=\frac{R^{\prime}}{p_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} \cdot c} \\ t:-a_{12}-2=0 & a_{12}=-2 \end{aligned} \] This is almost the same as the force coefficient except for a factor of 2 . Since multiplying by a number doesn't change the units of \( \Pi \), let \( 1-51 \) \[ \Pi_{1}=\frac{R^{\prime}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} c} C_{R^{\prime}}!_{\infty} \]
raw_text: Step 4: Form the non-dimensional products. Let \[ \Pi_{1}=\underbrace{\rho_{\infty}^{a_{11}} V_{\infty}^{a_{12}} c^{a_{13}}}_{\text {Reperty }} \underbrace{R^{\prime}} \text { Nu-Repertis. } \quad \text { Solve for } a_{11}, a_{12}, a_{13} \] \( \Pi_{1} \) is dimensionless, so each exponent must be zero: \[ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a l_{1}}\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{12}}(l)^{a_{13}}\left(\frac{m}{t^{2}}\right)=m^{\circ} l^{\circ} t^{\circ} \\ m: & a_{11}+1=0 \\ l:-3 a_{11}+a_{12}+a_{13}=0 \Longrightarrow & \Rightarrow a_{13}=-1 \quad \pi_{1}=\frac{R^{\prime}}{p_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} \cdot c} \\ t:-a_{12}-2=0 & a_{12}=-2 \end{aligned} \] This is almost the same as the force coefficient except for a factor of 2 . Since multiplying by a number doesn't change the units of \( \Pi \), let \( 1-51 \) \[ \Pi_{1}=\frac{R^{\prime}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho_{\infty} V_{\infty}^{2} c} C_{R^{\prime}}!_{\infty} \]

text: \[ a_{21}, a_{22}, a_{23} \neq a_{11}, a_{12}, a_{13} \] Let \( \quad \Pi_{2}=\underbrace{\rho_{\infty}^{a_{21}} V_{\infty}^{a_{22}} c^{a_{23}}}_{\text {some reperty }} \underbrace{\mu_{\infty}}_{\substack{2 t \\ \mu_{\infty}}} \) For \( \Pi_{2} \) to be non-dimensional we need: \[ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a_{11}}\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{22}}(l)^{a_{13}}\left(\frac{m}{l}\right)=m^{0} l^{0} t^{0} \\ m: & a_{21}+1=0 \quad a_{21}=-1 \\ l:=3 a_{21}+a_{22}+a_{23}-1=0 & \Rightarrow a_{23}:-1 \\ t:-a_{22}-1=0 & \prod_{22}=-1 \end{aligned} \] Thus, \( \Pi_{2}=\frac{\mu_{\infty}}{\rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} c} \) Similarly, we obtain \( \Pi_{3}=\frac{a_{\infty}}{V_{\infty}}=\frac{1}{M_{\infty}} \) The statement that we started with can be rewritten as \[ \begin{array}{l} R^{\prime}=f\left(\rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty}\right) \\ c_{R^{\prime}}=f_{1}\left(\frac{1}{\operatorname{Re}_{\infty}}, \frac{1}{M_{\infty}}\right)=f_{2}\left(\operatorname{Re}_{\infty}, M_{\infty}\right) \\ \text { Reciprocels are } 1! \end{array} \] \( 1-52 \)
raw_text: \[ a_{21}, a_{22}, a_{23} \neq a_{11}, a_{12}, a_{13} \] Let \( \quad \Pi_{2}=\underbrace{\rho_{\infty}^{a_{21}} V_{\infty}^{a_{22}} c^{a_{23}}}_{\text {some reperty }} \underbrace{\mu_{\infty}}_{\substack{2 t \\ \mu_{\infty}}} \) For \( \Pi_{2} \) to be non-dimensional we need: \[ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a_{11}}\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{22}}(l)^{a_{13}}\left(\frac{m}{l}\right)=m^{0} l^{0} t^{0} \\ m: & a_{21}+1=0 \quad a_{21}=-1 \\ l:=3 a_{21}+a_{22}+a_{23}-1=0 & \Rightarrow a_{23}:-1 \\ t:-a_{22}-1=0 & \prod_{22}=-1 \end{aligned} \] Thus, \( \Pi_{2}=\frac{\mu_{\infty}}{\rho_{\infty} V_{\infty} c} \) Similarly, we obtain \( \Pi_{3}=\frac{a_{\infty}}{V_{\infty}}=\frac{1}{M_{\infty}} \) The statement that we started with can be rewritten as \[ \begin{array}{l} R^{\prime}=f\left(\rho_{\infty}, V_{\infty}, c, \mu_{\infty}, a_{\infty}\right) \\ c_{R^{\prime}}=f_{1}\left(\frac{1}{\operatorname{Re}_{\infty}}, \frac{1}{M_{\infty}}\right)=f_{2}\left(\operatorname{Re}_{\infty}, M_{\infty}\right) \\ \text { Reciprocels are } 1! \end{array} \] \( 1-52 \)

text: AAE333 In-Class Example (Buckingham-Pi) An incompressible, Newtonian fluid flows through a long, smooth-walled horizontal pipe. Viscous effects result in a pressure drop inside the pipe along its length. The governing equation to calculate the pressure drop per unit length \( (\Delta P) \) is: \[ \Delta P=f(V, D, \rho, \mu) \] Where \( V \) is the fluid velocity, \( D \) is the pipe diameter, \( \rho \) is the fluid density, and \( \mu \) is the dynamic viscosity of the fluid. Use the Buckingham Pi method to calculate the nondimensional functional form. Use \( \Delta P \) and \( \mu \) as the non-repeating variables.
raw_text: AAE333 In-Class Example (Buckingham-Pi) An incompressible, Newtonian fluid flows through a long, smooth-walled horizontal pipe. Viscous effects result in a pressure drop inside the pipe along its length. The governing equation to calculate the pressure drop per unit length \( (\Delta P) \) is: \[ \Delta P=f(V, D, \rho, \mu) \] Where \( V \) is the fluid velocity, \( D \) is the pipe diameter, \( \rho \) is the fluid density, and \( \mu \) is the dynamic viscosity of the fluid. Use the Buckingham Pi method to calculate the nondimensional functional form. Use \( \Delta P \) and \( \mu \) as the non-repeating variables.

text: 4. Form our \( \pi \) terms and solve \[ \begin{aligned} \Pi_{1} & =D^{a_{11}} V^{a_{12}} \rho^{a_{13}} \cdot \Delta P e \\ m^{0} e^{0} t^{0} & =l^{a_{11}} \cdot\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{12}}\left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a_{13}} \frac{m}{l^{2} \cdot t^{2}} \end{aligned} \] \( m: 0=a_{13}+1 \quad a_{13}=-1 \) l: \( 0=a_{11}+a_{12}-3 a_{3}+1=a_{11}-2+3 t_{4}^{-2} \therefore a_{11}=t_{21} \) 1 \( t: 0=-a_{12}-2 \quad a_{12}=-2 \). \[ \pi_{1}=\frac{\Delta P e \cdot D}{\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} \] \[ \begin{aligned} \pi_{2} & =D^{a_{21}} V^{a_{22}} a^{a_{23}} \cdot \mu \\ m^{0} l^{0} t^{0} & =l^{a_{21}}\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{22}}\left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a_{23}} \cdot\left(\frac{m}{l \cdot t}\right) \end{aligned} \] \[ \begin{array}{l} m: 0=a_{23}+1 \quad a_{23}=-1 \\ l: 0=a_{21}+a_{22}-3 a_{23}-1=a_{21}-1+3-1=a_{21}+1 \therefore a_{21}=1 \\ t: 0=-a_{22}-1 \quad a_{22}=-1 \end{array} \] 5. Re-write statement \[ \frac{\Delta P_{e} \cdot D}{\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}=f\left(\frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right) \]
raw_text: 4. Form our \( \pi \) terms and solve \[ \begin{aligned} \Pi_{1} & =D^{a_{11}} V^{a_{12}} \rho^{a_{13}} \cdot \Delta P e \\ m^{0} e^{0} t^{0} & =l^{a_{11}} \cdot\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{12}}\left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a_{13}} \frac{m}{l^{2} \cdot t^{2}} \end{aligned} \] \( m: 0=a_{13}+1 \quad a_{13}=-1 \) l: \( 0=a_{11}+a_{12}-3 a_{3}+1=a_{11}-2+3 t_{4}^{-2} \therefore a_{11}=t_{21} \) 1 \( t: 0=-a_{12}-2 \quad a_{12}=-2 \). \[ \pi_{1}=\frac{\Delta P e \cdot D}{\rho V_{\infty}^{2}} \] \[ \begin{aligned} \pi_{2} & =D^{a_{21}} V^{a_{22}} a^{a_{23}} \cdot \mu \\ m^{0} l^{0} t^{0} & =l^{a_{21}}\left(\frac{l}{t}\right)^{a_{22}}\left(\frac{m}{l^{3}}\right)^{a_{23}} \cdot\left(\frac{m}{l \cdot t}\right) \end{aligned} \] \[ \begin{array}{l} m: 0=a_{23}+1 \quad a_{23}=-1 \\ l: 0=a_{21}+a_{22}-3 a_{23}-1=a_{21}-1+3-1=a_{21}+1 \therefore a_{21}=1 \\ t: 0=-a_{22}-1 \quad a_{22}=-1 \end{array} \] 5. Re-write statement \[ \frac{\Delta P_{e} \cdot D}{\rho V_{\infty}^{2}}=f\left(\frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right) \]

text: Some Practical Issues Remember: Blunt body= body where most of the drag is pressue dras Streamlined body = body where most of the drag is friction drag Blunt bodies have a much higher \( \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{D}} \) than streamlined bodies. The skin friction coefficient \( \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{f}} \) is higher for turbulent flows than laminar flows because/velocity profile is flatter: Explain: golf ball dimples, old baseball have smaller drag 1-37

text: Three important non-dimensional numbers Continuum vs free molecular flow Knudsen number: \( K n=\frac{\lambda}{L} \) where \( \quad \lambda= \) mean free path \( L= \) characteristic length at sea level \( \lambda \approx 60.80 \mathrm{~nm} \) at \( 100 \mathrm{~km} \quad \lambda=10 \mathrm{~cm} \) if \( \mathrm{Kn}<0.01 \quad \) continuum flow (Macroseopic Represent tur) \( \left.\begin{array}{ll}0.01<\mathrm{Kn}<10 & \text { rarefied } \\ \mathrm{Kn}>10 & \text { free molecule }\end{array}\right\} \) - Durt partnles in atmospher \( 1-38 \)

text: Incompressible vs compressible flow Mach number: \( \quad M=\frac{V_{\infty}}{a}=\frac{V_{\infty}}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}} \) \( a= \) speel of sound \( \gamma \) : cplev ( 1.4 for air) [] \( R= \) Gas Constat \( \left[\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{Lg} \mathrm{K}}\right] \) \( T= \) Tempertive [K] \( M_{\infty}<0.3 \) : density is nearly constant (Subsonic, Incompressible) \( M_{\infty}>0.3 \) : Compressible flow with Mach \# regimes: Subsonic (Compressible) \( \Rightarrow M_{\infty}<0.8 \) then \( M<1 \) everywhere Transonic \( \quad 0.8<M_{\infty}<1.2 \) Supersonic \( M_{\infty}>1.2 \quad M>1 \quad \) everywhere Hypersonic \( M_{\infty}>5.0 \) 1-39

text: Buckingham Pi Theorem The name Pi comes from the mathematical notation \( \Pi \), meaning a product of variables. Principle of Dimensional Homogeneity (PDH): Any equation describing a physical situation will only be true if both sides have the same units. \[ F=m \cdot a \quad[N]=[k g]\left[m / s^{2}\right] \] The Buckingham Pi Theorem states: If a physical process satisfies PDH and involves \( \boldsymbol{N} \) dimensional variables, it can be reduced to N-K non-dimensional variables or \( \Pi s \). The reduction \( \boldsymbol{K} \) is equal to the maximum number of variables that do not form a \( \Pi \) among themselves and is always less than or equal to the number of fundamental units (mass, length, time, etc) describing the variables. 1-41
raw_text: Buckingham Pi Theorem The name Pi comes from the mathematical notation \( \Pi \), meaning a product of variables. Principle of Dimensional Homogeneity (PDH): Any equation describing a physical situation will only be true if both sides have the same units. \[ F=m \cdot a \quad[N]=[k g]\left[m / s^{2}\right] \] The Buckingham Pi Theorem states: If a physical process satisfies PDH and involves \( \boldsymbol{N} \) dimensional variables, it can be reduced to N-K non-dimensional variables or \( \Pi s \). The reduction \( \boldsymbol{K} \) is equal to the maximum number of variables that do not form a \( \Pi \) among themselves and is always less than or equal to the number of fundamental units (mass, length, time, etc) describing the variables. 1-41

text: Method of Repeating Variables is based on the application of Buckingham Pi theorem to determine the non-dimensional parameters that can be formed from a group of dimensional variables describing a physical process. Step 1: List all the variables that are involved with the problem. The number of variables ( N ) describe a process which has a functional form of \( \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}}=f_{1}\left(\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}, \ldots, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}-1}\right) \). Step 2: Express each of the variables in terms of basic dimensions. Number of basic dimensions is K . Most common in fluid mechanics are mass ( M ), length ( L ), time (T), temperature, etc. Step 3: Determine the number of pi terms using the Buckingham Pi theorem ( \#П Terms = N-K). 1-42
